verdict_reasoning: TGFB1 is a secreted cytokine produced as a latent complex—the mature TGF-β dimer remains non-covalently bound to its prodomain (LAP, latency-associated peptide), which tethers it in an inactive, cell-surface-associated form via interaction with LTBP (latent TGF-β binding protein), an ECM-anchored TM or ECM-resident protein. The latent TGFB1 complex is deposited onto and remains stably associated with the cell surface and extracellular matrix during secretion. Additionally, TGFB1 reaches the outer leaflet transiently during cell-state contexts (activation-induced secretion, wound healing, cancer-cell secretion, and immune-cell infiltration). The protein is not constitutively membrane-anchored by a TM domain or GPI anchor; instead, it is stably surface-tethered as a component of the latent complex during regulated exocytosis and ECM deposition. This qualifies as dual_localization (surface-associated latent complex alongside soluble intracellular precursor) and cell_state_induced (activation-dependent secretion and surface retention).
predicted_key_uncertainty: The precise distinction between (a) TGFB1 as a stably surface-tethered component of the latent complex (qualifying for contextual / stable_surface_attachment or dual_localization) versus (b) TGFB1 as a secreted, matrix-deposited product in equilibrium with the soluble pool (qualifying for secreted_only) hinges on whether the latent-complex association with LTBP and cell-surface anchors is wash-resistant and represents a genuine surface-accessibility target. Literature supports stable extracellular tethering, but the degree to which the latent complex is 'accessible' from the outer face during its dormant state (versus only upon activation-induced release) is not entirely settled. Conservative interpretation favors contextual over yes, and dual_localization over stable_surface_attachment, because the active soluble form is released post-activation rather than pre-existing as a stable PM resident.
